region_of_origin: India (North India / Punjab); served widely in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Kadai Mutton; FOODON_03315595 (mutton dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (mutton, cooked dish) used as the closest defensible category; no Kadai-specific code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Kadai Mutton or a sufficiently close parent concept in the food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kadai Mutton is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous portion of mutton pieces in thick spiced gravy with bell peppers and tomatoes, usually accompanied by naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Kadai Mutton — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Kadai Mutton (record G00010383). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked mutton (NDB 17174) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this dish; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on mixed meat curry with vegetables
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kadai Mutton. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed meat curry with vegetables (GI ~35), which shares a comparable macronutrient profile: moderate protein, moderate fat, low-to-moderate carbohydrate, and spiced tomato-based gravy. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (35) × carbohydrate per serving (7.44 g/100g × 250 g serving = 18.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 6.5 g GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100g; potassium 210 mg/100g provides partial offset but sodium-to-potassium ratio remains unfavourable
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Kadai Mutton to a small portion (≤100 g) and request reduced-salt preparation, as a standard 250 g serving delivers approximately 1168 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — and the potassium content is insufficient to fully counteract this sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (7.44 g/100g) and low GI (~35); however, moderate-high fat (11.58 g/100g) and high sodium require portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kadai Mutton has a low glycaemic index (~35) and modest carbohydrate content, making it a reasonable protein-rich choice for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a controlled portion (≤150 g) and paired with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice or naan, which would substantially raise the overall glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~4.2 g/100g, cholesterol 85 mg/100g, sodium 467 mg/100g, and estimated trans fat 0.1 g/100g from animal fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume Kadai Mutton sparingly due to its combination of saturated fat (~4.2 g/100g), dietary cholesterol (85 mg/100g), high sodium (467 mg/100g), and trace trans fat from animal sources, all of which contribute to LDL elevation and arterial blood pressure; if consumed, portion size should not exceed 100 g and preparation should use minimal oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6); nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes) may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit Kadai Mutton, as mutton's high arachidonic acid content amplifies pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6), and the nightshade vegetables (bell peppers, tomatoes) in the dish may worsen joint inflammation in sensitive individuals; requesting preparation with less oil can reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadai Mutton (also spelled Karahi Mutton or Kadhai Mutton) is a popular North Indian and Pakistani meat dish cooked in a kadai — a thick, circular, deep cooking vessel resembling a wok — over high heat. The dish features bone-in or boneless mutton pieces simmered and stir-fried in a robust, semi-dry spiced tomato-and-onion gravy enriched with freshly ground coriander, cumin, red chilli, ginger, garlic, and garam masala. Bell peppers (capsicum) are a signature addition that lend a mild sweetness and crunch. The result is a deeply aromatic, moderately spicy dish with a thick, clingy sauce, tender mutton, and a characteristic charred edge from the high-heat wok cooking. In Singapore, Kadai Mutton appears on the menus of North Indian and Mughlai restaurants and is commonly served with naan, roti, or steamed basmati rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 467 mg. The dish is high in protein and has a low glycaemic index (~35), making it suitable in moderate portions for people managing blood glucose, but its sodium and saturated fat levels require caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free and halal when prepared with permissible ingredients.
common_user_queries: Is Kadai Mutton good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record G00010383, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy cooked mutton data as HPB does not separately report this field; this introduces minor uncertainty for that single field only. All other values are exact from HPB.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.2 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked mutton (NDB 17174) as the HPB Foodo record does not separately report saturated fat for this dish. This is the only field not directly sourced from HPB. 2. Trans fat (0.1 g/100g) is an estimate based on naturally occurring trans fat in ruminant animal fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid); no direct measurement is available from HPB or USDA for this specific dish preparation. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, most vitamins, and moisture data for this specific Kadai Mutton preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed meat curry with vegetables; no direct GI measurement for Kadai Mutton has been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. 5. Cultural classification: the input record listed 'Middle Eastern' as primary cuisine; this has been corrected to 'South Asian / Indian' as Kadai Mutton is a North Indian / Pakistani dish cooked in a kadai wok. The dish is served in Singapore and region_tags SG is retained. 6. Dietary flag is_halal set to true as mutton is permissible under Islamic dietary law when slaughtered according to halal standards; consumers should verify halal certification at point of purchase.
